nodes.content: A supreme being exists who is all-powerful, all-knowing, and all-good
A sacred text exists that is divinely inspired and is the ultimate authority on truth
Prayer and ritual connect humans to the divine and produce tangible results
Those who do not believe face negative eternal consequences
All morality originates from the supreme being — without the divine, there is no moral foundation
A specific historical figure performed miracles, conquered death, and this event is the cornerstone of truth
Always ask why. Follow reasoning chains to their root.
When two explanations exist, prefer the simpler one that requires fewer assumptions.
Test all claims against observable, measurable reality.
Trust moral intuitions — if something feels wrong, investigate why.
No claim is exempt from questioning. If something can't be questioned, question why it can't.
Love, sacrifice, and redemption are the supreme being's core attributes
The historical figure's tomb was found empty — no natural explanation suffices
Communities built around these beliefs produce transformed lives
The sacred text has been preserved accurately for thousands of years
The complexity and beauty of nature proves intelligent design
The moral law written on every heart points to a divine lawgiver
Millions of believers across centuries report personal experiences of the divine
Prophecies in the sacred text were fulfilled, proving divine authorship
The historical figure's followers willingly died rather than recant — proving their conviction
Suffering is a test of faith or consequence of human failing, not divine negligence
The sacred text is internally consistent and without error
Ask and it shall be given — the divine promises to answer prayer
Children die of cancer despite fervent prayer from believing parents
A devastating tsunami killed 230,000 people across 14 countries in 2004
Your closest neighbor is Hindu, deeply kind, generous, and morally admirable
Different denominations of the same religion violently disagree on fundamental doctrines
Thousands of other religions exist with equally convinced followers and similar miracle claims
Archaeological evidence contradicts several historical claims in the sacred text
Controlled scientific studies (STEP trial, 2006) show no measurable effect of prayer on outcomes
Former believers who leave the faith report equal or improved wellbeing and moral behavior
Grief hallucinations are well-documented in psychology — bereaved people commonly see/hear deceased loved ones
Secular communities (AA alternatives, humanist groups) produce equivalent transformation and belonging
The sacred text contains passages endorsing slavery, genocide, and subjugation of women
Charismatic leaders in documented modern cults produce identical conviction, sacrifice, and miracle claims
The STEP trial's null result directly contradicts the claim that prayer produces tangible results — if prayer worked as claimed, controlled studies would detect it
Children dying of cancer despite fervent prayer is not merely a failed test — it is a moral crisis for any claim of an all-good, all-powerful being who answers prayer
The existence of thousands of religions with equally convinced followers suggests that conviction and personal experience are unreliable indicators of truth
A deeply kind and morally admirable Hindu neighbor disproves the claim that morality requires the specific supreme being — moral excellence exists across all belief systems
Former believers reporting equal or improved wellbeing contradicts the claim that the divine is necessary for human flourishing
Sacred text passages endorsing slavery and genocide create an irreconcilable tension with the claim of an all-good deity as the source of morality
The 'moral lawgiver' argument commits a logical error: it assumes morality requires an external source rather than emerging from social evolution and empathy
Archaeological evidence contradicting the sacred text undermines the claim of divine authorship — an all-knowing author would not make historical errors
Denominational violence over doctrine proves the sacred text is NOT internally consistent — if it were clear and unified, sincere readers would not reach contradictory conclusions
Prophecy fulfillment claims require scrutiny: were prophecies specific enough to be falsifiable, or vague enough to be retrofitted to events?
Modern cult followers willingly die for beliefs we know to be false — willingness to die proves conviction, not truth
Grief hallucinations explain post-mortem appearances: bereaved people routinely see and hear deceased loved ones, no resurrection required
The empty tomb claim is not independently verifiable — it comes from the same texts whose other historical claims are contradicted by archaeology
Secular communities producing equivalent transformation proves that belonging and purpose — not divine intervention — drive personal change

The 'intelligent design' argument from beauty and complexity is undermined by the equal existence of parasites, childhood cancer, and natural disasters — design implies a designer indifferent to suffering
Religious communities do provide real psychological benefits — social bonding, ritual, shared meaning — these are genuine human goods even if the theology is false
The 230,000 tsunami deaths cannot coherently be attributed to 'human failing' — this is natural evil that an all-powerful being chose not to prevent
The 'suffering as test' theodicy is morally repugnant when applied to infants and children — they cannot be meaningfully 'tested'
If the supreme being's core attributes are love and redemption, the existence of eternal punishment for disbelief is a direct contradiction — infinite punishment for finite doubt is not love
The claim 'no claim is exempt from questioning' directly conflicts with faith-based epistemology, which treats foundational religious claims as beyond question

Personal religious experience is indistinguishable from experiences reported in every other religion and in documented psychological phenomena — it cannot be used as evidence for one specific theology
Asking 'why' about the supreme being's existence leads to an infinite regress — if everything needs a creator, who created the creator? Special pleading is required to stop the chain
Pattern recognition: every belief doctrine has at least one environment fact that contradicts or undermines it — the belief system is not robust against real-world evidence
The strongest pro-religion argument remaining is the subjective value of community and meaning — but this supports religion as useful fiction, not as truth

Occam's razor consistently favors naturalistic explanations: psychology explains religious experience, evolution explains morality, community explains transformation — no supernatural entity is needed
Some religious moral teachings (love your neighbor, care for the poor, practice forgiveness) have genuine ethical value independent of their claimed divine origin

After applying reasoning principles to all beliefs and environment facts, the foundational claim of an all-powerful, all-knowing, all-good supreme being faces irreconcilable contradictions with observed reality
The sacred text's authority collapses under examination: historical errors, moral atrocities, internal contradictions, and denominational disagreement all undermine divine authorship
The eternal consequences claim is the most ethically troubling: it weaponizes fear to prevent the very questioning that would reveal the system's contradictions

CONCLUSION: The religious belief system, when subjected to consistent reasoning principles, fails on empirical, logical, and moral grounds — not because of bias, but because the evidence and reasoning lead there
What survives: the human capacity for love, community, moral reasoning, and meaning-making does not require supernatural foundations — these are real and valuable on their own terms

The most honest position: extraordinary claims require extraordinary evidence. The religious claims are extraordinary; the evidence is ordinary — personal experience, ancient texts, and tradition, all of which exist equally in contradictory religions

STEP trial is a controlled test of prayer efficacy
Promise of answered prayer fails empirical test
Prayer producing tangible results is testable and fails
Applying: test claims against measurable reality
Direct observation of unanswered prayer in extreme need
If all-powerful and all-good, why allow children to suffer when parents pray?
The 'suffering as test' response feels morally inadequate for childhood cancer
Moral intuition: testing children with cancer is wrong
Multiple religions with identical conviction patterns
Personal experience is not unique to one religion
Simpler explanation: humans naturally generate religious experience
Direct observation of moral excellence outside the faith
Morality clearly exists without this specific divine foundation
The moral law points to human nature, not necessarily a specific lawgiver
Testing the moral origin claim against observable reality
Empirical data on post-deconversion wellbeing
If leaving faith improves life, negative eternal consequences seem unjust
Transformation is not unique to religious communities
Testing claims against measurable outcomes
Direct textual evidence of morally repugnant commands
If morality comes from this being, why does the text endorse immorality?
Love and redemption are contradicted by commanded genocide
Moral intuition: slavery and genocide are wrong regardless of source
Why must the moral law have a supernatural origin?
Asking why — following the reasoning chain to its root
Evolutionary morality requires fewer assumptions than a divine lawgiver
Archaeological contradictions are documented
Preservation is moot if the content contains errors
Internal consistency claim fails against external evidence
Can a text with factual errors be the ultimate authority on truth?
Denominations violently disagree on fundamentals
The text's own readers cannot agree on its meaning
How can a divinely clear text produce thousands of contradictory interpretations?
Applying critical analysis to prophecy claims
Asking why — what counts as genuine prophecy fulfillment?
Testing prophecy claims against standards of evidence
Documented cult martyrdom parallels
Dying for a belief is not evidence the belief is true
Simpler explanation: humans die for deeply held false beliefs regularly
Well-documented psychological phenomenon
Natural explanation exists for post-death sightings
If grief hallucinations are common, miracle claims need stronger evidence
Prefer simpler explanation requiring fewer assumptions
Source reliability matters for historical claims
If the text has historical errors, its unique claims lose credibility
Testing claims against observable reality — the source is not independently confirmed
Humanist groups produce same outcomes
Transformed lives are not evidence of divine truth
Simpler explanation: community itself transforms, not theology
Design argument is selective — ignores the horror in nature
Childhood cancer is part of 'design' too
Tsunamis killing 230,000 are part of 'design' too
Testing design claims against full observable reality, not cherry-picked beauty
Communities DO transform lives — the mechanism is social, not supernatural
Secular equivalents confirm the mechanism is community, not content
Observable: community works. The theological explanation is the extra assumption
Natural disaster with massive innocent casualties
The 'human failing' defense does not apply to tsunamis
All-powerful + all-good + tsunami deaths = logical tension
Testing infants is incoherent
Children with cancer are not being tested
Moral intuition: this framing is deeply wrong
Love contradicts eternal torment
Is eternal punishment compatible with an all-good being?
Moral intuition: eternal punishment for honest doubt is unjust
A kind Hindu neighbor faces eternal punishment? That feels deeply wrong
Applying the principle to religious claims
A text claiming ultimate authority resists questioning by definition
Following reasoning chains to their root
Personal experience is universal, not theology-specific
Other religions report identical experiences
Psychology explains these experiences naturally
Simpler: human brains generate religious experiences
What caused the uncaused cause?
Following reasoning to its root reveals the regress
Stopping the regress at 'God' is no simpler than stopping at 'universe'
Testing all claims against reality reveals systematic failure
Community benefit is real
But secular alternatives achieve the same benefit
Applying Occam's razor across all claims
The supreme being hypothesis adds complexity without explanatory gain
Love and sacrifice are genuinely good values
But their value doesn't require a divine source — they work because of human empathy
Moral intuition affirms these values while rejecting the packaging
The tri-omni God is incompatible with observed suffering
Children's cancer
Tsunami deaths
Tested against reality
Multiple lines of evidence against divine authority
Archaeological contradictions
Denominational disagreement
Morally repugnant content
Eternal punishment prevents honest inquiry
If questioning is forbidden, question why
Moral intuition: fear-based belief systems are coercive
Followed reasoning to root
Applied Occam's razor
Tested against reality
Trusted moral intuitions
Questioned everything
Love is real; its source need not be divine
Community transformation is real; the mechanism is human
The kind Hindu neighbor demonstrates morality without specific theology
Secular communities demonstrate meaning without religion
Miracle claims lack extraordinary evidence
Other religions have equal evidence base
The evidentiary standard is not met
